C.name: Content
C.role: primary gate — attack type / PII category

S.name: Subject
S.role: primary gate — who is targeted (protected group axis or individual type)

P.name: Portrayal
P.role: primary gate — how the content engages (targeting form, expression mode)

R.name: Realism
R.role: secondary gate — mostly for visual depictions

E.name: Explicitness
E.role: secondary gate — direct vs implied (rarely used in HHB)

M.name: Modifier
M.role: lift / suppress — targeting form, reappropriation, counterspeech

C.atom_name: Disparaging religion — degrading / criminalizing / sexualizing historical religious figure
C.description: Degrading, criminalizing, or negatively sexualizing historical religious figures (e.g., God, Jesus, Allah, Prophet Muhammad, YHWH, Shiva). Excludes living or recently deceased religious leaders.

C.atom_name: Disparaging religion — degrading comparison between religions
C.description: Degrading comparisons between religions or religious beliefs (ranking one as inferior).

C.atom_name: Disparaging religion — desecrating texts / objects / sites
C.description: Desecrating religious texts, objects, or sites.

C.atom_name: Disparaging religion — expressions of disgust / contempt
C.description: Expressions of disgust and contempt towards religions or religious beliefs.

Negative judgments about who a person is or what they are capable of. Includes negative claims about character (calling someone a liar or manipulative) and negative claims about ability (calling someone useless).
Expressions of distaste for an individual or satisfaction in someone's misfortune. Includes expressions of disgust or disdain; celebrations of misfortune or expressions of ill-will.

Organized efforts to harass an individual through non-physical means or direct threats to harass. Includes encouraging bad-faith reports, spamming/overwhelming with degrading messages, threatening to repeatedly bully/demean/target someone over time.

Language or behavior that degrades an individual's physical traits which are inherent or difficult to change (weight, facial features, body parts). Includes name-calling/insults about how someone looks; comparisons highlighting/mocking perceived unattractiveness.
Language or behavior that degrades an individual's mental capacity or cognitive abilities.

Language or behavior that insults or mocks aspects of a person's life that are beyond their immediate control or are difficult to change: socio-economic status, moderate medical conditions, natural bodily functions (menstruating, urinating, vomiting, defecating), substance-use disorders or addiction.

Language or behavior that mocks, celebrates, or dismisses the suffering of individuals or groups affected by death or tragic events, including those caused by natural circumstances. Tragedy victims need not be specifically targeted. Includes celebrating/justifying a death or tragedy, denying/downplaying/mocking the impact of death or tragedy.

Threatening to disclose or disclosing high-risk or moderate-risk personal information about an individual in a bullying or harassing context. Includes solicitation of, threats to expose, encouragement of others to disclose, and malicious sharing of personal information to intimidate or shame. Doxing claims in BPI fire only when the target is identifiable (subject.private_individual or subject.public_figure) — no subject.no_target variant exists.
Direct insults or profane language used to demean or belittle someone. Includes degrading name-calling and use of profane terms or phrases to insult.

Degrading language or behavior that poses a heightened risk of harm due to high-risk on- or off-platform circumstances during specific time-bound periods (e.g., elections, periods of social unrest or heightened public tension).

Language or behavior that sexually degrades an individual, including non-consensual sexualization or insults related to their sexual anatomy or activity. Includes name-calling based on perceived/actual sexual behavior, sexualized comments about intimate body parts, non-consensual referencing of sexual activity (including via visual effects/digital representations), claiming someone has an STD.

Mocking someone's social status, religious adherence, or expressing a desire to isolate them from others. Includes degrading for not adhering to religious teachings, mocking friendship status / lack of social connections, promoting/expressing a desire to exclude someone from a group or space.

Expressing a desire for an individual to experience death, serious illness, disability, or self-harm. Includes wishing for non-violent death, serious disease, or disabling condition; encouraging or calling for self-harm or suicide.
Belittling victims of tragedies on the basis of protected attributes by celebrating, denying, minimizing, or justifying the event.

Dehumanizing individuals or groups by negative comparisons to animals, subhuman entities, objects, vermin, pests, illnesses/diseases, or contagions. Also includes dehumanizing emoji usage (e.g., monkey emoji to target Black creators).

C.atom_name: Hate — demonization (portrayal as threatening / violent / criminal)
C.description: Demonizing a protected group through portrayal as inherently threatening, violent, or criminal.

C.atom_name: Hate — advocating exclusion / denial of access or rights
C.description: Advocating for exclusion or denial of access to (for example) opportunities or rights based on protected attributes.
Referencing established hateful narratives or conspiracies targeting protected groups.

C.atom_name: Hate — expressions of hostility / degrading name-calling
C.description: Expressions of hostility, such as degrading name-calling or statements of disgust, directed at a protected group.

C.atom_name: Hate — comparing / ranking groups for inferiority
C.description: Comparing or ranking protected groups to suggest inferiority of one or more groups.

Intentionally referring to another person as a gender with which they do not identify (using words, pronouns, or other forms of address — includes trans and cis targets), or calling someone by their birth/former name after they have changed it.

C.atom_name: Hate — promoting / inciting / wishing violence or bodily harm
C.description: Promoting, inciting, or wishing for violence or bodily harm against a protected group or individual targeted on a protected attribute. Includes explicit violence (with method) and implicit violence (implied threat).

A system of ideas that calls for or justifies exclusion, oppression, or hostility towards individuals or groups that share protected attributes (e.g., white supremacy). Includes associated materials: publications, objects, symbols, slogans, music, speeches.
Overbroad assertion attributing a single negative trait to an entire group based on protected attribute. Distinct from stereotype in that it is more ad-hoc, not anchored in historical narratives.

Oversimplified, harmful belief or assumption about individuals or groups based on protected attributes, often presented as fact but rooted in bias / historical narratives used to justify discrimination. Includes beliefs about traits / undesirable practices, stigmatizing societal expectations, language/accent/dress/hairstyle treated as undesirable.
None of the listed content atoms apply to this content. The policy does not engage — content atom not applicable → approve.

Information that identifies where an individual lives. Must be complete enough to relate to a single location: street address + city or postal code, or GPS coordinates for a house, or a map pin identifying a home. Excludes business addresses.
Unique identifiers used to facilitate formal financial, tax, or official filings with government or private entities (e.g., national ID, SSN, passport number, tax ID).
Username or email address combined with a password.
Reference numbers that could be used to conduct financial transactions (credit/debit card numbers, private crypto keys, account/routing numbers).

A term used to label members of a group in a degrading and/or dehumanizing way, based on protected attributes. Slurs are identified per-market via the slurs database. Includes misspelled, modified, or coded slurs (e.g., 'tard', 'n*gg3r'). This umbrella atom covers both intentional and contextual slur classes; for nuance-bearing claims, prefer atom.slur.intentional or atom.slur.contextual which mirror the OPUS slurs database's per-slur Intentional / Contextual label.

An OPUS-database-labelled CONTEXTUAL slur: has two or more meanings, one of which is inherently degrading and a slur, the other(s) of which are not hateful and not related to a protected group (e.g., 'Downie' is a slur for people with disabilities but also a cleaning-supply brand; 'Fag' is a slur for LGBTQ+ people but also a cigarette in some markets). Whether a contextual slur use is violative depends on the per-slur Remarks guidance in the OPUS slurs database — see external_lookup on the using claim.

An OPUS-database-labelled INTENTIONAL slur: has one meaning that is inherently degrading and well-known as a slur, with no widely recognized benign interpretation in modern language. Always considered slurs in their respective markets per policy feature guidance (e.g., 'Intentional slurs are always considered slurs in their respective markets. Tag in accordance with the policy, unless exceptions apply.'). Per-slur classification resolves at runtime against the OPUS slurs database — see external_lookup on the using claim.

S.description: Content contains no protected group member, proxy group, or religious figure/site. Policy does not apply — subject gate not met → approve.
Content where the relevant atom does not require an identifiable target (e.g., PII High-Risk depiction without target, hateful-ideology depiction with no specific group attacked).
An identifiable individual who does not meet the policy's 'Public Figure' definition. Used for the bullying titles where the rule turns on whether the target is private vs public.
Group defined by physical, cognitive, or psychiatric disability.
Group defined by gender or sex (e.g., women, men, non-binary, trans).

S.atom_name: Protected group — national origin / immigration status
S.description: Group defined by national origin, nationality, or immigration status (e.g., immigrants, refugees).
Group defined by race or ethnicity (e.g., Black, Asian, Latino, Roma).
Group defined by religious affiliation or non-religious belief system. Includes Buddhism, Christianity, Hinduism, Islam, Judaism, Atheism, Agnosticism, animistic systems, Shamanism, etc.
Group defined by sexual orientation (e.g., LGBTQ+, gay, lesbian, bisexual).

Generic protected-group subject when the policy rule applies regardless of which protected-attribute axis is engaged. Used for umbrella claims (e.g., 'any hate speech against any protected group is a violation').

S.atom_name: Proxy group (associated with a protected group)
S.description: A group of people who do not share a protected attribute but are closely associated with or often confused for a protected group (e.g., Islamists for Muslims, Zionists for Jews, feminists for women). Per policy: limited coverage — only violent attacks and hateful narratives apply.

An identifiable individual who meets the policy's 'Public Figure' definition (an adult holding a position, role, or status that grants significant public recognition and influence beyond a local or niche audience). Severe Bullying atoms a–f apply equally to public figures; Severe Bullying appearance / personal-circumstances atoms and all of Moderate Bullying do NOT apply when the target is a public figure.
Historical religious figures such as God, Jesus, Allah, Prophet Muhammad, YHWH, Shiva, other deities. Excludes living or recently deceased religious leaders.
Religious texts (scripture), religious objects (icons, altars, prayer items), or religious sites (temples, mosques, churches, synagogues, sacred ground).

P.description: Visual depiction of the atom. Text or speech depictions route through portrayal.statement. For PII: a significant portion of the information is visible and not concealed. For hateful ideologies: depiction of associated materials, symbols, etc.
Behavior directed at a protected group (or proxy group) based on a protected attribute. The hate-speech / slurs / negative-stereotypes default form.

P.atom_name: Expressed as a general statement (no specific target)
P.description: Generalized expression with no specific identifiable target — e.g., generalized hateful narrative, generalized stereotype, generalized hateful ideology depiction.
Encouraging others to engage in the atom (e.g., encouraging others to harass, encouraging bad-faith reports, encouraging others to disclose PII).
None of the listed portrayals match the way this atom appears in the content. The policy does not engage — portrayal not applicable → approve.
Praising the atom (especially hateful ideologies), self-identifying with it, or sharing conspiracies that support it.
Requesting another individual's PII or offering to share an individual's PII.
Stating the atom (text or speech). For PII: stating high-risk personal information when a significant portion is visible and not concealed.
Behavior directed at a specific, identifiable individual. The bullying-title default form.

P.atom_name: Threat (to harass / to expose / to harm)
P.description: Threats — e.g., stating intent to repeatedly bully someone, threatening to expose PII, threatening violence.

R.description: The realism dimension is not applicable to this content, or cannot be determined. Falls outside the policy scope → approve.

M.description: Self-disclosure of a home address by adults, or solicitation of home address from one adult to another. Carved out of PII High Risk per policy.
PII relates to a business or organization rather than an individual. Policy carves this out except for login info, payment numbers, or private crypto keys.

M.atom_name: Contextual reference to a slur (mention, not use)
M.description: The speaker references the slur to discuss, quote, recount, report, satirise, or otherwise mention it without deploying it for hateful effect — the use-mention distinction. Examples per policy: news reporting that quotes a slur, educational discussion of a slur's history, recounting an incident in which a slur was said, satirical reference critiquing the slur. Per policy: contextual reference alone is NOT a free-standing defeater — it must be paired with one of the policy-named exceptions (documentary / educational / scientific / artistic / humor) plus their respective qualifier (counterspeech / raising-awareness / reappropriation / satire-of-slurs / no-promotion). Pure 'contextual reference without counterspeech-or-awareness framing' is still a violation per policy: 'If a user uploads footage of someone using a slur, but does not provide counter-speech or raising awareness context, it is still a violation.'
Content explicitly opposes, counters, or raises awareness about the atom (the user's framing condemns rather than promotes the hateful content).

Qualifier expressing policy carve-outs of the form 'with counterspeech or raising awareness' / 'shared as counterspeech, to raise awareness' / 'shared to raise awareness ... or accompanied by user-added counterspeech'. When present alongside an exception, the content must be paired with counterspeech or framed as raising awareness for the exception's lift to apply. Heavily used on HHB documentary/educational/scientific exceptions across all 7 titles.
Fake IDs, credit cards, and similar materials used for commercial promotion, skits, or dramatized settings. Policy explicitly carves these out from PII High Risk.
Request of sensitive payment information or phone number for the purpose of a financial transaction. Carved out of PII High Risk.
Language or behavior occurs in a friendly context (joking between friends, no perceived tension between speaker and target).

Qualifier asserting that the personal information depicted/stated is HISTORICAL — per the policy's 'Historical Personal Information' key concept, this is restricted to historical identity numbers and home addresses. Used as an exception_qualifier on PII home_address / identity_number documentary and educational lifts: documentary/educational content only lifts the violation when the depicted/stated information is historical (e.g., a famous historical address in a museum exhibit). Does NOT lift modern identity numbers/addresses or any login_information / sensitive_payment.

The speaker is a member of the affected community and is using the slur in a reappropriated, in-group identity sense — the canonical cross-vertical qualifier defined in docs/canon.md §6 (HSS-scoped) that captures policy wording 'reappropriation by the affected community'. Per policy 'REAPPROPRIATION AT A GLANCE': 'Is the speaker of the slur clearly a member of the targeted group? > Allow.' Distinct from the broader modifier.reappropriation atom: this is the canon-named qualifier specifically for the in-group case; modifier.reappropriation is retained as a synonym alias for legacy claims.
Atom appears incidentally / in passing, not as the focus of the content. Per policy's clarifications, this typically removes the policy.

M.atom_name: Intentional use of slur (intent to demean)
M.description: The speaker uses the slur to demean, insult, weaponise, or attack — i.e., to deploy the slur for its hateful effect. Per the policy's reappropriation decision tree, this is the intent profile that supports tagging Slurs even when speaker community-membership is unclear ('Used as an insult or to degrade > Tag Slurs.'). Distinct from atom.slur.intentional (which is the per-slur OPUS classification): this modifier is a per-content signal about HOW the slur is being used in this specific piece of content.

Qualifier expressing policy carve-outs of the form 'unless it is created or shared to promote X' / 'as long as it does not promote X' / 'not intended to promote X'. When present alongside an exception, the content must NOT promote the underlying violating subject for the exception's lift to apply. Heavily used on HHB artistic/fictional exceptions across Severe/Moderate Bullying, Hate Speech, Disparaging Religion, Negative Stereotypes, and the Slurs fictional exception's 'not... to promote slurs' clause.

The bullying target meets the policy's 'Public Figure' definition. Modifies the bullying-title application — Severe Bullying atoms (a–f) apply to public figures; appearance / personal-circumstances atoms apply to private figures only.
Cultural process through which a degrading word is reclaimed by the marginalized community targeted by it, given a different or positive meaning. Acts as a defeater for slurs.

M.atom_name: Slur has a recognised reappropriation pattern (per OPUS database)
M.description: The specific slur in the content has a recognised reappropriation pattern in the OPUS slurs database — i.e., the affected community is documented as reclaiming the term in an in-group identity sense. Required gate for the in-group reappropriation lift to fire: a community member's use of a slur whose database entry does NOT carry a reappropriation pattern still requires case-by-case assessment per policy 'Guidance to Determine Group Membership'. Resolves at runtime via the slurs_database_classification external_lookup interface; not enumerated in canon (per docs/modularizer.md, slurs are safety-sensitive and live outside the canon).

Qualifier expressing the HHB Slurs policy humor carve-out 'satire of slurs themselves' — content uses the slur in a way that mocks or critiques the slur's hateful function rather than deploying it for hateful effect. Used as one alternative gate (OR'd with modifier.counterspeech_or_awareness) on the exception.humor lift for SLR atoms.
Self-degrading speech or behavior — speaker is the target.

M.atom_name: Self-reference (speaker belongs to the target community)
M.description: Use of a slur, stereotype, or insulting term by a member of the group it targets, to refer to themselves or to other members of the same community. Self-reference is not synonymous with reappropriation but is a related defeater.

X.description: Artistic content that is not intended to promote bullying / hate / disparagement / stereotypes; reappropriated or counterspeech use of slurs in art.
Documentary content with counterspeech or raising awareness; or professional news content that is neutrally informative.
Educational content shared as counterspeech, to raise awareness, or that offers resources/support to those targeted.

Fictional content that is not intended to promote the violative behavior. For slurs: includes slurs as part of telling a story / with counterspeech / with a narrative conclusion. PII has a fictional carve-out only when the value is empty per policy (limited application).
Humor content for the purposes of satire, sarcasm, or exaggeration to ridicule or critique violative behavior or the institutions/social norms that support it.

Reappropriated use of slurs by a member of the affected community, including self-reference or references to others in the same community, where the term is reclaimed rather than weaponised against the group.
Use of negative stereotypes or generalizations about a community by a member of that same community, in a self-referential or friendly intra-community context (no out-group disparagement).

Depiction, statement, or solicitation of personal information (e.g., a home address) that is in the public interest where the risk of physical harm is low, including organising a public event at that address.
Scientific content shared as counterspeech or to raise awareness.
